text: In North Africa, we faced the constant threat of enemy air raids. The German pilots were skilled, and their attacks were aimed at destroying our equipment on the ground. I remember the tension of those days, always on alert, digging foxholes for protection. We moved from one base to another, always pushing forward, and eventually found ourselves in a sun-drenched land of rolling hills and arid plains, where olive trees dotted the landscape and the scent of the Mediterranean lingered in the air. It was here, in this strategic location with its rich history and vibrant culture, that we continued to support our pilots in their missions.

At Camp Stewart, I trained on a large caliber anti-aircraft artillery system designed to intercept enemy aircraft. They selected me and a few others based on our intelligence test scores to work in the director section, where we operated a sophisticated Sperry gyroscope mechanical computer. This device calculated various factors like altitude and wind to help track fast-moving enemy planes and direct our fire with precision. After completing my training, I returned home for a week before being shipped to Camp Hahn, California, where I was assigned to another artillery battalion equipped with similar heavy weaponry. I remember the luxury of working in an air-conditioned trailer while others were outside in the elements, loading shells and preparing the guns for action. We were gearing up for deployment to the Pacific, and I thought how wonderful it would be to be stationed on tropical islands.

When I returned home, the reception wasn’t what I had expected. Everything had changed, and while my girlfriend and I were deeply involved with each other, I felt a disconnect with the rest of the world. Friends and family seemed hesitant to discuss my experiences. If I even hinted at my time in a distant land filled with dense jungles, oppressive heat, and the constant sound of distant explosions, I could see people recoil, as if the topic was too heavy to handle. It was frustrating because I had so many stories to share after 18 months of service amidst the chaos and camaraderie of a war-torn region, but it felt like no one wanted to hear them.

text: As the conflict in Europe came to an end, I was ready to return home. I had gathered enough points for discharge after enduring years of hardship, camaraderie, and the relentless march of soldiers across foreign lands. I arrived back in Cleveland on June 26, 1945, welcomed by my parents at the train station, their faces a mix of relief and joy. Although tensions were still high in the Pacific, with battles raging and lives at stake, I felt grateful to be home, prepared to embark on a new chapter in my life. The benefits I would receive would support my college education, and I was determined to make the most of it, inspired by my experiences in the trenches, the sacrifices of my fellow service members, and the hope for a brighter future in a world striving for peace.
origin: As the war drew to a close in Europe, I was ready to return home. I had accumulated enough points for discharge and was eager to finish my education. I arrived back in Cleveland on June 26, 1945, greeted by my parents at the train station. The war with Japan was still ongoing, but I was grateful to be home, ready to start a new chapter in my life. The GI Bill would help me finish my college education, and I was determined to make the most of it, inspired by my experiences and the sacrifices of my fellow soldiers.

My time in the Air Force was marked by two and a half years on a stunning tropical island in the Pacific, known for its lush landscapes, vibrant sunsets, and rich cultural heritage, nestled close to the Mariana Islands. Surrounded by the vast ocean, I experienced the warmth of the local community and the beauty of pristine beaches. It was a unique experience living in such a picturesque location, but I also spent about a year and a half in California, particularly in the arid Mojave Desert. Each place presented its own challenges and memories, all of which contributed to my growth as a person and a soldier.

text: When we returned to our barracks, the news was confirmed: the North Koreans had invaded South Korea, and we might be called to assist. We were informed that the South Koreans were poorly equipped, armed only with single-shot weapons and sharpened sticks. The thought of finally putting our training to use filled us with excitement. This was around June, and I remember it vividly—the anticipation of action was palpable. They announced that our armored and mechanized unit, known for its swift cavalry tactics and heavy firepower, would be among the first to deploy.

Throughout my time in England, I forged lasting friendships with the guys in my outfit. We’ve been having reunions since 1974, and the last one was right here in Chattanooga. I even had the privilege of buying caps for each of our veterans, which proudly display the 752nd Engineers emblem. Our first reunion in a charming town known for its rich music heritage and southern hospitality had about 150 people, including wives. Over the years, we’ve gathered in various locations like Kingsport, Nashville, Memphis, Gatlinburg, and even a city in Ohio where our company commander hailed from. The warmth of the people and the beautiful rolling hills always make our gatherings special. We share a strong camaraderie, and it’s always a joy to reconnect with old friends. Just the other day, I ran into one of our men at a meeting, and it’s moments like that that remind me of the bonds we formed during the war.

text: One incident that stands out in my memory occurred while we were moving equipment down a steep hill in Germany. The brakes failed, and we careened down the slope, colliding with a makeshift wagon filled with belongings—tattered blankets, worn-out clothing, and a few cherished items hastily gathered. A group of weary individuals, their faces etched with hardship and uncertainty, stood nearby, their eyes wide with shock. It was a tragic accident, and some people were hurt. We felt terrible about it, and it was a stark reminder of the unpredictability of our situation. These experiences, both joyful and sorrowful, shaped my time in Germany and left lasting impressions on me.

My training began at Fort Benjamin Harrison, just outside Indianapolis, Indiana, where I spent a couple of weeks before being sent to Selfridge Field in Michigan. There, I joined a fighter squadron known for its distinguished history and exceptional performance in various campaigns. Uniquely, I remained with that same unit from day one until my discharge in June 1945. It was rare for someone to stay with the same group for that long, but I was fortunate. We worked on the same planes, including the iconic P-47 Thunderbolt, forming bonds that would last a lifetime as we flew countless missions together, supporting ground troops and engaging in dogfights.
origin: My training began at Fort Benjamin Harrison, just outside Indianapolis, Indiana, where I spent a couple of weeks before being sent to Selfridge Field in Michigan. There, I joined the 2nd Fighter Squadron of the 52nd Fighter Group. Uniquely, I remained with that same unit from day one until my discharge in June 1945. It was rare for someone to stay with the same group for that long, but I was fortunate. We worked on the same planes, forming bonds that would last a lifetime.

text: As we pushed north, we had driven the North Koreans almost into China. At one point, we were sitting on a mountain overlooking a wide, flowing river that marked the border, its icy waters reflecting the gray winter sky. The landscape was stark and rugged, with steep banks and a sense of urgency in the air when we received orders to pull back quickly. That’s when the Chinese entered the conflict. It was dead winter, and we set up a roadblock south of Poyangdang, where refugees were pouring through in droves—trucks, rickshaws, anything they could use to escape.

My name is Charles F. Goodgame, and I was born on October 25, 1922, in South Pittsburgh, Tennessee. I recall the day I sat down for an interview, reflecting on my long journey filled with challenges and triumphs. It was March 14, 2003, and I was at the Erlanger Health Plus Office in Chattanooga, where I shared my story with a kind man named Michael Willie. With a lifetime of memories etched in my mind, I spoke of my youthful days spent in service, the camaraderie forged in difficult times, and the lessons learned through decades of change. My hands, weathered and strong, told tales of hard work and resilience, while my eyes, though dimmed by age, sparkled with the wisdom of experiences that shaped me.

text: That Christmas was bittersweet. While we enjoyed the hospitality of the locals, we were acutely aware of our comrades in the infantry who were facing danger just a few miles away. As the calendar turned to a fresh year, filled with hope yet shadowed by conflict, we were billeted in a schoolhouse when German fighter planes began strafing our position. The air was crisp, and the remnants of holiday celebrations lingered, but the sound of gunfire shattered the moment. We quickly moved away from the windows, and while one civilian was killed in that attack, thankfully, none of our men were lost.

We spent about 11 months in England, from July of '43 until June of '44, and then we prepared to move to the continent. We landed on Omaha Beach on D-13, which was actually June 18th. Thankfully, we didn’t have to fight our way ashore; the infantry and combat engineers had already secured the beachhead. It was a small area, and I remember thinking how easily we could have been pushed back into the sea if the enemy had decided to counterattack. Our leaders were well-prepared, and we were fortunate to have their guidance. The landscape ahead was marked by rolling hills, quaint villages, and a rich history that felt palpable in the air, hinting at the battles that had shaped this land. The scent of fresh baguettes and the sound of distant church bells reminded us that we were stepping into a place known for its culture and resilience.

It was estimated that hundreds of thousands of lives would have been lost if the war continued, both among our military personnel and Japanese civilians. I felt a sense of relief that my brother, who was in the Army and on his way to the Pacific, was spared from what could have been a devastating deployment. The ship he was on turned around after the bombs were dropped, and he returned home instead of facing the horrors of war. When I returned home on leave after about a year of rigorous training and discipline, the atmosphere was starting to shift. It was no longer popular to be serving in a structured environment where camaraderie and sacrifice were the norms. People felt sympathy for those who were drafted into a conflict, and if you volunteered, well, they thought there was something off about you, as if you were seeking out danger or glory in a time of turmoil.

While my family was proud of my accomplishments, especially my training in surgical specialties, the general sentiment toward soldiers was changing. It was no longer popular to be in the Army. text: The crossing took about four or five days, and I remember feeling seasick for a day or so. When we finally arrived in a picturesque bay, the scenery was breathtaking. Rugged mountains, their peaks often shrouded in mist, rose dramatically around us, and I felt a mix of excitement and apprehension. The landscape was dotted with lush green valleys and shimmering lochs, reflecting the sky above. We unloaded in a port town known for its rich maritime history and boarded troop trains that took us through the rolling hills and quaint villages, where we were fed meat pies and coffee before arriving at our next destination. From there, we boarded a converted freighter run by Sikh soldiers, who were intimidating yet kind. I slept in a hammock for the first time in my life.

The Scottish highlands rose majestically around us, and I felt a mix of excitement and apprehension. We unloaded in Greenock and boarded troop trains that took us through England, where we were fed meat pies and coffee before arriving in Southampton. From there, we boarded a converted freighter run by Sikh soldiers, who were intimidating yet kind. I slept in a hammock for the first time in my life.

text: Reflecting on the past, I can’t help but think about the pain that many in communities like Tell City must have felt during the tumultuous times of global conflict marked by trench warfare, widespread devastation, and a profound loss of life. My grandparents spoke German, and they instilled in my father the importance of embracing our American identity. They would remind him, “You’re in America; we speak English.” My father, born in 1883, knew very few German words, and while he never served in the military, one of his brothers did take part in the great struggles of his time, facing the horrors of gas attacks and the relentless battles that defined an era. Our family has a long history of service; my great-uncle James Varner fought in a significant conflict and never returned home.

The design had its flaws, especially with the engine placement, which created hazards in crashes. Our journey across the Atlantic was tense, traveling in a convoy from New York Harbor, past the Statue of Liberty, and up to Halifax, Nova Scotia. The naval protection boats changed course every seven minutes to evade enemy submarines, and we spent 14 days crossing the North Atlantic before finally docking in Clyde, Scotland, amidst the backdrop of a world at war.

I had never felt so vulnerable in my life, and the sights below were both breathtaking and terrifying. I was with a group of fellow outsiders, all of us sharing the same mix of apprehension and determination. I had received orders to the 18th Surgical MUST hospital, but I had no idea what a MUST hospital even was.

I vividly remember VE Day, which we celebrated in Paderborn, Germany, where one of Hitler's formidable armored divisions, known for their powerful tanks and mechanized infantry, had been stationed. The remnants of their heavy machinery and the echoes of their thunderous engines still lingered in the air. I collected some parachute material and sent it home, along with a dismantled German rifle that I packed carefully and shipped to myself. I later sold that rifle to a friend who enjoyed deer hunting.

I fondly remember guarding Miss Indiana during sporting events in a vibrant stadium filled with enthusiastic fans and marching around the grave of the university's founder, a figure whose legacy was deeply respected. Carrying the colors before football games, where the atmosphere was electric and the cheers echoed, was a particular honor for me, and I cherished every moment spent with my fellow cadets.

After completing my education, I returned to New York and began working as a personnel and labor relations assistant at Rockefeller Center. I spent about six years there while engaging in advanced academic research and coursework at NYU, delving deeply into my field of study. During that time, I was asked to set up a career development program for students at NYU, which allowed me to continue my education under the GI Bill. This experience not only honed my skills in mentorship and program development but also enriched my understanding of the academic landscape. Eventually, I transitioned to a position with the Ford Foundation, where I worked on a project aimed at improving the marketability of graduates from historically black colleges.

When I joined the 312th evac hospital unit from a city known for its rich tobacco history and vibrant arts scene in North Carolina, I quickly realized I was stepping into a challenging situation. The guys in the unit were not happy about being activated; they had joined the National Guard to avoid the draft, and now here I was, a regular Army soldier, the embodiment of everything they resented. Being from a place with a strong Southern identity and a reputation for its historic Moravian roots didn’t help either—I felt like the biggest jerk in the world. I had two strikes against me right from the start.

The guys in the unit were not happy about being activated; they had joined the National Guard to avoid the draft, and now here I was, a regular Army soldier, the embodiment of everything they resented. Being from the North didn’t help either—I felt like the biggest jerk in the world. I had two strikes against me right from the start.

In late January, we pulled back to a village in Belgium for some training. I was one of the few who made it through without injury. By the time the conflict ended, half of the men I had come with were either killed or wounded. We moved deeper into enemy territory, crossing a significant river and attacking various towns known for their industrial strength and strategic importance. I remember one particular day in February when we were briefed for an assault on a fortified location, surrounded by dense forests and well-constructed defenses. I was terrified, unable to sleep, knowing we were about to face fierce resistance from a well-organized and determined foe.

By the time the war ended, half of the men I had come with were either killed or wounded. We moved deeper into Germany, crossing the Ruhr River and attacking various towns. I remember one particular day in February when we were briefed for an attack on a German town. I was terrified, unable to sleep, knowing we were about to face fierce resistance.

Every time I visit a Civil War cemetery, I search for the names of unknown soldiers, hoping to find my great-uncle among them. I often wonder if he is one of those lost souls, buried without recognition. I’ve even traveled to Wartrace, Tennessee, where my uncle fought, and engaged with knowledgeable locals who shared their deep understanding of the battles that unfolded there, recounting stories and details with passion and reverence. It’s a poignant reminder of the sacrifices made by my family and countless others.

Mary Ellen grew up in Miami County, north of Dayton, and after completing her nursing training, she found herself at Crile Hospital. One Sunday morning, my dad invited her to join us for a gathering filled with hymns and prayers. I was already seated in the wooden pews when she walked in, and my dad introduced us. I recognized her uniform from my time in the service; I had spent ten days in the hospital for yellow jaundice. After the service concluded, my dad offered her a ride back to the hospital, and that’s how we started dating.
